name: Vienna Game
blurb: 1.e4 e5 2.Nc3 — develop, hold e4, and keep the f2–f4 break in reserve. An aggressive, principled White repertoire built around the Vienna Gambit.

lines.name: Vienna Gambit — Main Line
whys: 1.e4 — grab the centre and free the bishop and queen. The backbone of every aggressive repertoire.
1…e5 — Black stakes an equal claim in the centre.
2.Nc3 — the Vienna. Develop, defend e4, and keep f2–f4 in reserve so the centre stays flexible.
3.f4 — the Vienna Gambit. Hit e5 and open the f-file for a kingside attack before Black is castled.
3…d5 — the critical reply. Black counter-strikes in the centre instead of grabbing on f4.
4.fxe5 — take, and the e5-pawn now drives the f6-knight; the centre opens in your favour.
4…Nxe4 — Black recovers the pawn, but the knight on e4 is active and a little loose.
5.Nf3 — develop with tempo, prepare d4, and cover e5/g5. (5.Qf3 is the sharp alternative.)
5…Be7 — Black develops solidly and prepares to castle.
6.d4 — build the broad centre. The e5-pawn cramps Black and the c1-bishop springs to life.
6…O-O — Black castles. You have a strong centre and natural pressure down the f-file.
lines.idea: f4 rips open the f-file; after …d5 fxe5 Nxe4, build with Nf3 and d4 for a powerful centre and kingside attack.

lines.name: Vienna Gambit — 5.Qf3 (Sharp)
whys: 1.e4 — open lines and seize the centre.
2.Nc3 — the Vienna; defend e4 and keep f4 ready.
3.f4 — the gambit: pry open the f-file.
4.fxe5 — capture; the knight on f6 must move and Black's centre comes under fire.
4…Nxe4 — Black wins the pawn back on e4.
5.Qf3 — double-attack: hit the e4-knight AND eye f7. The most forcing try.
5…Nxc3 — Black trades off the attacked knight before it's lost.
6.bxc3 — recapture toward the centre; the half-open b-file and big pawn centre favour White.
6…Be7 — Black develops and braces to castle.
7.d4 — claim the full centre; with the open f-file and bishop pair, White is comfortably better.
lines.idea: 5.Qf3 hits the e4-knight and f7 at once. After …Nxc3 bxc3, White gets a strong centre, the bishop pair, and the open f-file.

lines.name: Vienna Gambit — 5…Bg4
whys: 5…Bg4 — Black pins the f3-knight to add pressure on e5.
6.Qe2 — break the pin's sting: defend e4-ideas, prepare to castle, and leave the e4-knight hanging.
6…Nxc3 — Black trades before the knight drops.
7.dxc3 — recapture, open the d-file for the queen and free the c1-bishop. White is comfortable with the better structure.
lines.idea: Meet the …Bg4 pin with Qe2: the e4-knight is loose, and after the trade White opens lines with a healthy pull.

lines.name: Vienna Gambit — 5…Bc5
whys: 5…Bc5 — Black aims the bishop at f2, discouraging an early O-O.
6.d4 — gain a tempo by hitting the c5-bishop while building the centre.
6…Bb6 — the bishop retreats but stays active on the a7–g1 diagonal.
7.Bd3 — challenge the strong e4-knight and prepare to castle; the big centre gives White the edge.
lines.idea: Against …Bc5, play d4 with tempo and Bd3 to challenge the e4-knight — your centre and development outweigh Black's bishop.

lines.name: Vienna Gambit — Accepted (3…exf4)
whys: 4.e5 — the point: gain space and kick the f6-knight before Black is organised.
4…Ng8 — the knight is shoved all the way back; Black has lost development time.
5.Nf3 — develop, eye the loose f4-pawn and the kingside.
5…d6 — Black strikes at the e5-spearhead to free his game.
6.d4 — support the e5-wedge and complete a dominant centre. Black is cramped and badly behind in development while White has a big space edge and the f-file — full value for the pawn.
lines.idea: Accept the gambit with the e5! push — kicking the knight back and building a big centre with Nf3/d4; the lead in space and development outweighs the pawn.

lines.name: Vienna Gambit — Accepted, …g5 Hold
whys: 4.Nf3 — develop and stop …Qh4+ ideas while eyeing the loose f4-pawn.
4…g5 — Black grimly tries to hold the extra pawn with the pawn chain.
5.h4 — strike at the over-extended g5-pawn before the chain solidifies.
5…g4 — Black pushes on, but this loosens his kingside fatally.
6.Ng5 — leap into g5; the knight eyes f7 and h7 with Black's king stuck in the centre.
6…h6 — Black tries to evict the knight.
7.Nxf7 — the classic sacrifice: rip open f7 and win material or the king. This is why grabbing-and-holding the f-pawn is dangerous for Black.
lines.idea: If Black clings to the gambit pawn with …g5, blast it open with h4 and the Ng5–xf7 sacrifice — the king is too exposed.

lines.name: Vienna Gambit — Declined (4.d3)
whys: 4.d3 — the calm, sound option: reinforce e4 and keep control rather than enter the wildest theory.
5.Bxf4 — develop the bishop to an active square and open the f-file.
5…dxe4 — Black opens the centre and offers a queen trade.
6.dxe4 — recapture; the position simplifies with a pleasant, easy game for White.
7.Kxd1 — recapture with the king; the endgame is comfortable — active bishop, open f-file, and the safer structure.
lines.idea: Prefer a quieter game? 4.d3 keeps a firm grip on e4; after the central trades White has an easy, slightly better position.

lines.name: Vienna — Bishop's Setup, 3…Nxe4 4.Qh5!
whys: 3.Bc4 — the Bishop's setup: aim at f7 and keep d3 or f4 in reserve.
3…Nxe4? — the tempting but flawed grab. The knight wins a pawn but exposes the king.
4.Qh5! — the refutation: double-attack on e5 and (mate on) f7.
4…Nd6 — the only move: block the queen's path to f7 and hit the c4-bishop.
5.Bb3 — keep the strong bishop aimed at f7 instead of trading it.
5…Nc6 — Black defends e5 with the knight.
6.Nb5 — pile onto the weak d6/c7 squares; Black is tangled up and White regains the pawn with a big initiative.
lines.idea: Punish the greedy 3…Nxe4 with 4.Qh5! — the double attack on e5 and f7 leaves Black's pieces hanging and his king exposed.

lines.name: Vienna — Bishop's Setup, 3…Nc6
whys: 3…Nc6 — Black develops solidly and declines the e4-grab.
4.d3 — support e4 and open the c1-bishop; a flexible, sound centre.
5.Nge2 — unpin smartly: re-route the knight toward g3/f5 and keep e4 solid.
6.exd5 — meet the central break head-on, before Black gets in …d4 or …dxe4 with tempo.
7.O-O — castle to safety; the c4-bishop eyes d5/f7 and White has a sound, slightly freer game with f4 and a3/Bd2 still to come.
lines.idea: If Black avoids the …Nxe4 trap, play the quiet d3/Nge2 setup and meet …d5 with exd5 — a sound, slightly freer game with f4 in reserve.

lines.name: Frankenstein–Dracula Variation
whys: 4.Qh5 — double attack on e5 and f7.
4…Nd6 — block f7 and hit the bishop.
5.Bb3 — keep the bishop trained on f7.
6.Nb5 — hit the d6-knight and the c7-square.
7.Qf3 — keep the queen active, renewing pressure on f7 and the long light squares.
7…f5 — Black blocks the diagonal but weakens e6/e5.
8.Qd5 — the famous re-route: the queen lands on d5, forking the c6-knight and hitting f7/c7. White emerges on top from the chaos.
lines.idea: The sharpest Bishop's-setup line. After 3…Nxe4 4.Qh5 Nd6 5.Bb3, the Nb5/Qf3–d5 manoeuvre wins material amid the complications.

lines.name: Vienna — 2…Nc6 3.Bc4 Bc5
whys: 3.Bc4 — eye f7 and prepare a kingside build-up.
4.d3 — solidify e4 and open the c1-bishop.
4…d6 — Black props up e5 and frees his light bishop.
5.Nf3 — develop and add pressure to e5; here an early f4 is premature, so build soundly instead.
6.O-O — castle into a healthy Italian-type middlegame; the f4-break and the Nd5 jump stay in reserve for the right moment.
lines.idea: Against …Nc6/…Bc5, don't rush f4 — play d3 and develop soundly with Nf3/O-O, keeping the f4-break and the Nd5 jump for when they really bite.

lines.name: Vienna — 2…Nc6 3.Bc4 Nf6
whys: 4.d3 — keep e4 solid; a sound Italian-like structure.
5.Bg5 — pin back: pile pressure on f6 to soften Black's grip on e4.
6.Bxf6 — take; the trade hands you the bishop pair and damages nothing of yours.
6…Qxf6 — Black recaptures with the queen; White follows with Nd5, hitting the queen and c7 with tempo.
lines.idea: Meet …Nc6/…Nf6 with d3 and Bg5; the Bxf6 trade plus Nd5 gives White the bishop pair and tempo-gaining play on c7/e7.

lines.name: Vienna — 2…Bc5 3.Nf3
whys: 2…Bc5 — Black develops the bishop to its most active diagonal.
3.Nf3 — develop and immediately pressure e5; the most natural, principled reply.
4.Bc4 — train the bishop on f7 and prepare to castle; a sound Italian-type set-up.
5.d3 — keep e4 firm and open the c1-bishop.
6.O-O — castle into a healthy, slightly more comfortable middlegame, with the f4-break and Nd5 ideas in reserve.
lines.idea: Against the early …Bc5, develop naturally with Nf3 and Bc4 into a sound Italian-type set-up — keeping f4 and Nd5 in reserve.

lines.name: Vienna Gambit — 2…Nc6 3.f4 d6
whys: 3…d6 — Black supports e5 instead of taking; solid but passive.
4.Nf3 — develop and add a second attacker to e5.
4…exf4 — Black takes now that e5 is pressured.
5.d4 — seize the full centre and open lines toward Black's king; with a big lead in development and the half-open f-file, White has more than enough for the pawn.
lines.idea: If Black props up e5 with …d6, develop with Nf3 and seize the centre with d4 — the lead in development and the open f-file outweigh the gambit pawn.

lines.name: Vienna — Bishop's Setup, …Bc5
whys: 3…Bc5 — Black develops symmetrically, aiming at f2.
4.Nf3 — develop and pressure e5 — calm, principled, and sidestepping any …Nxe4 tricks.
5.d3 — keep e4 solid and open the c1-bishop.
6.O-O — castle into a sound Italian-type middlegame; the f4-break and the Nd5 jump are there for later.
lines.idea: If Black meets the Bishop's setup with …Bc5, develop calmly with Nf3/d3/O-O into a solid Italian-type game — keeping f4 and Nd5 in reserve.

lines.name: Vienna — 2…d6
whys: 2…d6 — a passive Philidor-like set-up that concedes central space.
3.f4 — punish the passivity by opening the centre at once.
5.d4 — take the whole centre while Black is cramped.
6.Bxf4 — recover the pawn with a beautifully active bishop; White enjoys a big centre and a clear space advantage.
lines.idea: Against the passive …d6, strike with f4 and seize the centre with d4 — then Bxf4 regains the pawn with a dominant position.

lines.name: Vienna — 2…g6 (Modern)
whys: 2…g6 — Black fianchettoes, but spends a move not contesting the centre.
3.f4 — open the centre before the fianchetto bishop is effective.
5.d4 — build the dominant centre that blunts the g7-bishop.
6.Bxf4 — regain the pawn actively; with the big centre, White has a comfortable space edge and easy development.
lines.idea: If Black fianchettoes with …g6, open the centre with f4/d4 — a broad pawn centre takes the sting out of the g7-bishop.

lines.name: Vienna Gambit — Main with Qe2
whys: 6.Qe2 — a flexible alternative to d4: pressure the e4-knight, prepare O-O-O, and ready a fast kingside roll. The knight must move.
7.dxc3 — open the d-file for the queen and bishop; White's lead in development and open lines promise the initiative.
lines.idea: A flexible main-line try: Qe2 hits the e4-knight, readies O-O-O, and after …Nxc3 dxc3 White opens lines with an initiative.

lines.name: Vienna — 2…Nc6 3.g3 (Mieses)
whys: 3.g3 — the calm Mieses set-up: fianchetto to bear down on the long diagonal and d5/e4.
4.Bg2 — the bishop eyes the centre and queenside; a safe, strategic plan if you prefer manoeuvring to the gambit.
5.Nge2 — flexible: the knight supports d4/f4 breaks and doesn't block the bishop.
6.O-O — castle safely; White has a sound, flexible position aiming for d4 or f4 at the right moment.
lines.idea: Prefer strategy over the gambit? The Mieses g3/Bg2 set-up is rock-solid and keeps the f4/d4 breaks for later.

lines.name: Vienna Gambit — Accepted, …Ne7–g6
whys: 4.e5 — push, kicking the knight and grabbing space.
4…Ng8 — the knight retreats; Black is undeveloped.
5…Ne7 — Black re-routes the knight toward g6 to hit the f4/e5 chain.
7.h4! — the punch: chase the g6-knight (h5 comes next) and start a kingside storm while Black is undeveloped. White keeps the e5-wedge and a raging initiative.
lines.idea: In the Accepted line, e5! then Nf3/d4; when Black hits f4 with …Ng6, blast forward with h4–h5 — the lead in development makes the attack crash through.

lines.name: Vienna — Bishop's Setup, quiet …Be7
whys: 3…Be7 — Black develops modestly and avoids the …Nxe4 complications.
4.d3 — keep e4 firm and open the c1-bishop.
6.O-O — castle; you reach a healthy Italian-type middlegame where the f4-break and Nd5 jump give White the more pleasant game.
lines.idea: If Black plays it safe with …Be7, transpose to a sound Italian-style setup with d3/Nf3/O-O — keeping f4 and Nd5 in reserve.

lines.name: Vienna Gambit — Main, …Bg4 & Be2
whys: 7.Be2 — calmly break the pin and prepare O-O; the e5-wedge and open f-file keep White on top.
lines.idea: In the main line, meet a later …Bg4 with Be2 — unpin, castle, and lean on the e5-wedge and the half-open f-file.
